title: Executive Order 11958—Administration of Arms Export Controls

flags.justification: Authority is distributed primarily between State and Defense by specific statutory section, with Treasury and Commerce receiving narrower slices, and extensive mandatory cross-consultation requirements throughout.
Extremely detailed, section-by-section delegation with required concurrence obligations built in (e.g., Sec. 1(l)(1) requires State's designations to have Defense's concurrence).
Comprehensive implementation of one statute (the Arms Export Control Act) across its many sections, all coherently serving one purpose.

Sec. 1(a)'s proviso lets the Secretary of State find whether a proposed transfer to an otherwise-ineligible country 'will strengthen the security of the United States and promote world peace' — a genuinely open-ended judgment standard for this specific exception category.
No incentive structure rewarding behavior contrary to the order's purpose is identifiable.
Standing implementation of a permanent statute; this order itself supersedes a prior delegation order (EO 11501), evidencing periodic revision.
The extensive mandatory cross-consultation and concurrence requirements throughout are added checks, not removed ones.

Extensive mandatory cross-agency consultation and concurrence requirements (State, Defense, Treasury, and the Arms Control and Disarmament Agency checking each other across different provisions) provide real, structured internal checks.
Routine, comprehensive delegation of an already-established statutory framework; not a novel authority claim.
The extensive cross-consultation and concurrence design throughout is a carefully constructed, conflict-avoiding distribution of authority.
No coverage population or exemption structure is created.

notes: Validation batch (positive). Structural weight: 1/20 = 5.0%. Issued two days before the Ford-to-Carter transition; a heavily cross-checked, well-designed delegation order despite its complexity and comprehensive scope.
